abstract:
In recent years, deep learning has experienced explosive growth, extending its utility into non-Euclidean domains such as graph-structured data. Because graphs represent complex relational information between elements, they have become a foundational data format for modern machine learning tasks. This paper explores the methodology of identifying nodes and edges within specific contexts—such as text, entities, and relations—to construct functional graph structures. Crucially, the choice of a Graph Neural Network (GNN) model often depends on the specific requirements of the application. 

By facilitating message passing, GNNs allow nodes to exchange information and capture the intricate dependencies inherent in both vertices and their connections. This study provides an in-depth analysis of prominent architectures, including Graph Convolutional Networks (GCNs), GraphSAGE, and Graph Attention Networks (GATs). We examine the underlying message-passing mechanisms of these models while evaluating their relative strengths and limitations across various domains. Furthermore, this paper provides a comprehensive map of the current landscape by detailing common GNN datasets, supporting Python libraries, and diverse real-world applications. This review serves as an extensive guide to the current state of GNN research and its practical implementation.
